coredata.dc:title: Cadmium induced aggregation of orange–red emissive carbon dots with enhanced fluorescence for intracellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Yalan
Wang, Chan
Jiang, Tao
Ran, Guoxia
Song, Qijun
coredata.dc:description: Cadmium is a notorious toxic heavy metal, that poses serious threat to human health. Sensitive and selective detection of cadmium in cells is of great significance in poison screening and disease diagnosis. Orange–red emissive carbon dots (OR-CDs), prepared from the calcination of selected carbon sources 5-amino-1, 10-phenanthroline (Aphen) and salicylic acid (SA), were found to act as a “turn on” type fluorescence probe for Cd2+ detection. The structure and optical properties of OR-CDs were comprehensively investigated by both experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The OR-CDs consist of a basic unit of nine aromatic rings, and the N/O binding sites on the OR-CDs can specifically bind with Cd2+, leading to aggregation induced enhanced emission (AIEE). A detection limit of 0.30 μM was achieved for Cd2+ with a linear range of 0.80–100 μM. OR-CDs can not only be used for intracellular Cd2+ imaging but also have the potential to alleviate cadmium poison in living organisms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Aggregation
Cadmium
Phenanthroline
Salicylic acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DFT optimization of the OR-CDs structure
The mechanism for the OR-CDs response to Cd2+
In vitro imaging and apoptosis analysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural National Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

School of Chemical and Material Engineering

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by Natural National Science Foundation of China (51973083), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (JUFSTR20180301). We would like to acknowledge the work of Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

This work is supported by Natural National Science Foundation of China ( 51973083 ), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology ( JUFSTR20180301 ). We would like to acknowledge the work of Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of OR-CDs (inset: the HRTEM of OR-CDs). (B) AFM image of OR-CDs (inset: the height profile of OR-CDs measured along the line shown). (C) TGA curve obtained for OR-CDs at a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1 under a N2 atmosphere. (D) DSC thermogram obtained for OR-CDs at a heating rate of 10 ℃ min−1 under a N2 atmosphere. (E) The FT-IR spectrum of OR-CDs. (F–H) High-resolution C1s, N1s and O1s XPS spectra for the OR-CDs.

(A) UV–vis absorbance spectrum for the OR-CDs; inset: photograph of OR-CDs under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV (right) irradiation. (B) 3D fluorescence spectra for OR-CDs. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra measured for OR-CDs in different solvents; inset: photograph of OR-CDs in different solvents under 365 nm UV light. (from right to left, the solvents are water, dimethyl sulfoxide, acetonitrile, dimethyl formamide, pyridine, acetone, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, methylbenzene and hexane). (D) Fluorescence emission spectra for 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs in the presence of 0–100 μM Cd2+, (inset: photographs of OR-CDs with various Cd2+concentrations under a 365 nm UV lamp). (E) Changes in the fluorescence intensity of the OR-CDs solution versus Cd2+ concentrations ranging from 0.8 to 100 μM (F) Influence of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs. The test metal ions include 30 μM Ag+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Al3+ or Cr3+.
(A) The optimized basic units range from five to ten conjugated rings based on DFT calculations. (B) The possible formation process for OR-CDs.
The formation of the Cd-OR-CDs aggregate.

(A) Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells. The scale bar is 100 µm, the concentration of OR-CDs is 0.10 mg/mL, and the concentration of Cd2+ is 20 μM. (B–E) Flow cytometric analysis of apoptotic/necrotic A549 cells. (B) A549 cells untreated with Cd2+ and OR-CDs. (C) A549 cells cultured with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs. (D) A549 cells treated with 20 μM Cd2+. (E) A549 cells co-cultured with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs and 20 μM Cd2+. (F) A summary of the apoptosis results.
The formation of OR-CDs and response to Cd2+.

head.ce:title: Cadmium induced aggregation of orange–red emissive carbon dots with enhanced fluorescence for intracellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Biological Colloids, Ministry of Education, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cadmium is a notorious toxic heavy metal, that poses serious threat to human health. Sensitive and selective detection of cadmium in cells is of great significance in poison screening and disease diagnosis. Orange–red emissive carbon dots (OR-CDs), prepared from the calcination of selected carbon sources 5-amino-1, 10-phenanthroline (Aphen) and salicylic acid (SA), were found to act as a “turn on” type fluorescence probe for Cd2+ detection. The structure and optical properties of OR-CDs were comprehensively investigated by both experimental characterizations and density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The OR-CDs consist of a basic unit of nine aromatic rings, and the N/O binding sites on the OR-CDs can specifically bind with Cd2+, leading to aggregation induced enhanced emission (AIEE). A detection limit of 0.30 μM was achieved for Cd2+ with a linear range of 0.80–100 μM. OR-CDs can not only be used for intracellular Cd2+ imaging but also have the potential to alleviate cadmium poison in living organisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Cadmium is an important transition metal that is widely used in industry, agriculture and military affairs (Bhamore et al., 2021). With the increasing use of cadmium, its direct or indirect threat to human health has become of great concern (Ayub et al., 2019). Many studies have shown that cadmium can easily accumulate in the human kidney, liver, lungs and bones with a long half-life of approximately thirty years, causing a number of serious diseases, such as osteoporosis, renal dysfunction, nervous system disorders and cancers (Dehghani et al., 2018; Ravichandiran et al., 2020). Therefore, the detection of Cd2+ is of great significance in poison screening, disease diagnosis and in environmental monitoring.

Among various analytical methods, fluorescent probes have emerged as a very attractive technique due to their high sensitivity, fast response, low cost, and suitability for in situ and living organism analysis. A number of fluorescent probes based on small organic molecules have been reported for Cd2+ detection. However, the preparations of small molecular probes usually involve complicated synthesis and purification procedures, and the obtained probes tend to have poor optical stability and often undergo photobleaching during prolonged light exposure (Qiu et al., 2019). Some small molecular probes also show poor water solubility; thus, they may not be readily applicable for the detection of Cd2+ in cells. In contrast, carbon dots (CDs) are usually prepared from simple hydrothermal or calcination methods (Jafari et al., 2021), and they often show substantially improved photostability (Jafari et al., 2022), low cost, good biocompatibility and highly efficient fluorescence emission (Xiao et al., 2020). Hence, CDs have become promising fluorescence probes for biological imaging and sensing (Lv et al., 2019; Shi et al., 2021). For example, nitrogen and sulfur codoped CDs were prepared for the detection of Cd2+ based on their quenching effect on the fluorescence CDs (Li et al., 2012; Gu et al., 2018). More recently, nitrogen and phosphorus codoped CDs were reported as “turn-on” fluorescence probe for Cd2+ detection (Lin et al., 2019); however, such CDs exhibit blue emission, and the short excitation wavelength can result in damage to cells and interfere with the auto-fluorescence of cells. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of reported CDs to Cd2+ is only described at the experimental level, and the mechanism for the change in fluorescence induced by Cd2+ has not been fully exploited.

Compared with turn-off-type fluorescent probes, turn-on-type probes are more attractive, which is not only convenient for virtual observation but can also substantially reduce interference from background noise (Mosca et al., 2015). In this regard, the aggregation-induced enhanced emission (AIEE) mechanism has great potential for the development of highly sensitive “turn-on” type probes (Ravichandiran et al., 2021a). The restricted intramolecular vibration and rotation in the aggregated state is believed to be responsible for the inhibition of the nonradiative process and enhanced radiation transition. A number of fluorescence turn-on probes have been developed based on the metal-induced AIEE effect for the molecules; however, this strategy has been rarely used for CDs-based fluorescence probes, and no fluorescence CDs probe based on the AIEE mechanism has been reported thus far.

Another frequently encountered problem in metal detection is interference from coexisting species in real samples. Many transition metals have similar binding properties (Ravichandiran et al., 2021b), and interference from neighbor metals of the periodic table is often inevitable (Li et al., 2012; Pu et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2017). In this respect, the Cd2+ fluorescence probes developed thus far are still subjected to interference from metals such as Zn2+ and Fe3+; consequently masking agents are required to alleviate their influences (Ravichandiran et al., 2021c; Tang et al., 2019). Hence, the development of novel fluorescence sensors with desirable specificity for Cd2+ detection is still urgently required (Ravichandiran et al., 2021d). If a size-based chelation can be introduced to the sensing mechanism, the selectivity for metal ion detection can be substantially improved.

In this work, a strategy based on metal-induced aggregation of CDs is considered to construct a turn-on-type fluorescence probe for Cd2+ sensing and imaging. Luminescent CDs were prepared by choosing nitrogen-containing aromatic precursors and appropriate linker molecules to achieve specific binding to Cd2+. As shown in Scheme 1, Aphen and SA were selected to synthesize orange–red emissive carbon dots (OR-CDs) with a long emission wavelength (605 nm). We have demonstrated for the first time that a strong aggregation process can be induced specifically by Cd2+ via its sterically restricted complexation with functional groups on OR-CDs. The as-prepared OR-CDs are successfully developed as a “turn on” fluorescence probe for the detection of Cd2+ in aqueous solution as well as for intracellular Cd2+ imaging.

Salicylic acid (SA) was acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 5-amino-1, 10-phenanthroline (Aphen) was purchased from Alpha Reagent Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). MgCl2.6H2O, CaCl2, BaCl2.2H2O, AlCl3.6H2O, MnCl2.4H2O, FeCl2.4H2O, FeCl3.6H2O, ZnCl2, CdCl2•2.5H2O, CrCl3.6H2O, PbCl2, NiCl2.6H2O, CoCl2.6H2O, AgCl, CuCl2.2H2O, ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), NaHPO4 and Na2HPO4 were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich, USA. The cytotoxicity detection kit CCK-8 and apoptosis kit were purchased from Beyotime Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was purchased from China Pharmaceutical Group Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. A dialysis membrane with a MWCO of 3500 Da was obtained from Yuanye Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

OR-CDs were synthesized by a simple calcination method (Chen et al., 2020). Briefly, 0.025 g Aphen and 0.5 g SA were thoroughly mixed before heating in a tube furnace to 200 °C at a rate of 5 °C min−1. The temperature was kept at 200 °C for 2 h. After natural cooling, the resultant products were dissolved in ultrapure water and then dialyzed through a 3500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the OR-CDs powders were acquired by lyophilization, and the OR-CDs powders were dissolved in ultrapure water for further testing.

To study the fluorescence response of the OR-CDs towards Cd2+, different amounts of Cd2+ solution were added to 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixtures was measured with an excitation wavelength of 460 nm. Acetic acid and sodium acetate buffer were used to maintain the pH (6.0) of the solution. The selective response of OR-CDs to Cd2+ was evaluated in the coexistence of various metal ions, including Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+ or Cr3+. The fluorescence intensity of OR-CDs in the absence (F0) or presence (F) of metal ions was recorded accordingly.

The Cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) was used to study the cytotoxicity of the OR-CDs towards A549 cells. Briefly, A549 cells along with different concentrations of OR-CDs (0 mg/mL, 0.01 mg/mL, 0.02 mg/mL, 0.04 mg/mL, 0.06 mg/mL, 0.08 mg/mL, 0.10 mg/mL and 0.20 mg/mL) and DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum were cultured for 24 h in 96-well plates at 37 °C in a humidification incubator containing 5% CO2. Then A549 cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) to remove the free OR-CDs. After adding 10 μL CCK-8 and 90 μL DMEM to each well, the cells were further incubated for 1.5 h at 37 °C. Cell viability was evaluated based on the absorbance measured at a wavelength of 450 nm.

For cellular imaging, OR-CDs (0.10 mg/mL) and A549 cells were initially incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. Then an appropriate amount of Cd2+ was added, and the mixture was further cultured for 30 min. After washing with PBS, images of the cells were taken by confocal laser scanning microscopy.

For the apoptosis experiment, A549 cells were divided into four groups: untreated cells, cells treated with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs, cells treated with 20 μM Cd2+ and cells treated with both 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs and 20 μM Cd2+. The above four groups of A549 cells were cultured under the same conditions for 24 h. Subsequently, the collected cells were stained with 5 μL annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (Annexin V-FITC) and 5 μL propidium iodide (PI). The percentage of apoptotic cells was evaluated by flow cytometry (Sarkar et al., 2017). The scatter plot of double variables was divided into four regions: necrotic cells (Q1, Annexin V-FITC negative, PI positive), late apoptotic cells (Q2, both Annexin V-FITC and PI positive), early apoptotic cells (Q3, Annexin V-FITC positive, PI negative) and intact cells (Q4, both Annexin V-FITC and PI negative).

In the calcination process, precursor molecules were cross-linked, dehydrated and carbonized to generate a carbon core and molecular fluorescent groups, i.e., the luminescent centers of the CDs. It is desirable that the CDs not only retain the functional group chelated with the metal ions but also form the long-wavelength emission luminescence center. Bearing this consideration in mind, Aphen was selected as the basic carbon source because it not only has a strong chelating ability for metal ions but also has a large planar aromatic structure. When other nitrogenous aromatic precursors instead of Aphen were mixed with SA for CDs preparation, the resultant CDs showed blue fluorescence and no enhanced response to Cd2+ (Fig. S1). When other precursors containing carboxyl groups instead of SA were mixed with Aphen for CDs preparation, the CDs prepared by aliphatic precursors had no fluorescence emission (Fig. S2A–C). A distinct black insoluble substance was produced in the preparation of CDs-5, which showed no fluorescence under a UV lamp, suggesting that the precursor was overcarbonized (Fig. S3). CDs-6 and CDs-7 are clear solutions in the daylight, and they also show no fluorescence in UV light, indicating that fluorescent CDs cannot be obtained by calcination of aliphatic precursors. A possible reason for this is aliphatic precursors are easily decomposed before the formation of conjugated luminescence centers in the carbonization process. Therefore, precursors with aromatic rings were selected as linker molecules to acquire CDs. When para- and meta-substituted aromatic carboxylic acids and Aphen were used, however, the produced CDs only exhibited green fluorescence (Fig. S2D–I), presumably because the steric hindrance of these precursors prevents the formation of large conjugated structures. After meticulous selection of the precursors, CDs with long-wavelength luminescence centers were obtained by SA and Aphen (Fig. S4) and the obtained OR-CDs show an obvious enhanced fluorescence response to Cd2+.

The morphology of OR-CDs was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). As shown in Fig. 1A and S5, the OR-CDs display homogeneous dispersion with an average size of 2.46 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shown in the inset of Fig. 1A does not show obvious lattice spacing, revealing the existence of amorphous carbon core (Tong et al., 2015). Correspondingly, the AFM image of OR-CDs in Fig. 1B also exhibits a narrow particle size distribution with a mean height of 1.87 nm. These results show that the OR-CDs show quasi-spherical morphology. The broad peak at 23° in the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern is ascribed to the (002) plane of amorphous carbon (Fig. S6) (Wang et al., 2020). Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to explore the thermal stability of the OR-CDs, and the results are presented in Fig. 1C. Two-step decomposition processes are observed, and the first degradation step observed at 220–310 ℃ is ascribed to decomposition from thermally unstable organic moieties (Mehta et al., 2014). The second degradation step at 310–460 ℃ should result from the thermally more stable species in aggregated cores. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) curve in Fig. 1D displays a large endothermic transition within the temperature range of 60–90 °C, which can be attributed to the breakdown of intramolecular hydrogen bonds (Long et al., 2018). Thus, the OR-CDs can be viewed as the product of aggregation of many amorphous carbon cores that associate together through hydrogen bonds of functional groups on the core surfaces.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were carried out to further investigate the functional groups in the OR-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1E, the sharp peaks at 1653 cm−1 and 1484 cm−1 are in accord with the CO stretching and N-H bending of amides, respectively (Qu and Sun, 2020). The stretching vibration of O-H appears at 3234 cm−1 and the CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group is found at 1715 cm−1 (Wu et al., 2018). The XPS survey in Fig. S7 shows that there are three characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 399 eV, and 532 eV, indicating that OR-CDs are mainly composed of C (83.14%), N (6.50%), and O (10.36%), respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum for C 1 s (Fig. 1F) is deconvoluted into three-unit moieties: CC/C-C (284.8 eV), C-O (285.58 eV) and CO (288.69 eV) (Gong et al., 2016). As shown in Fig. 1G, the N 1 s spectrum for OR-CDs can be resolved into two peaks at 398.77 eV and 400.5 eV, which are ascribed to pyridinic N and N-H, respectively (Ren et al., 2018). The high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum exhibits distinctive peaks due to CO, C-O and OC-O at 530.71 eV, 531.95 eV and 533.29 eV, respectively (Fig. 1H) (Jiang et al., 2020). The surface components of the OR-CDs shown in the XPS results are in good agreement with the FT-IR data, indicating the coverage of hydrophilic oxygen functional groups and nitrogen functional groups on the surface of the OR-CDs.

The as-prepared OR-CDs exhibit distinctive optical properties in the UV-Vis and fluorescence spectra. As shown in Fig. 2A, the UV-Vis absorption spectra for the OR-CDs display three main peaks at 232 nm, 294 nm and 353 nm, which correspond to the n–σ* transitions (C-OH, C-NH), π-π* transitions (CC) and n-π * transitions (CO, CN), respectively (Wang et al., 2018). The illustration in Fig. 2A shows that OR-CDs are a yellow solution under daylight and emit orange–red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. The OR-CDs appear as yellow powders under daylight and exhibit orange–red fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S8). In the 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 2B), the maximum emission wavelength is located at 605 nm with a quantum yield (QY) of 27%. Additionally, the OR-CDs exhibit excitation-independent behavior, which can be attributed to the molecular state in the OR-CDs (Soni et al., 2021). To prove this, the influence of different solvents on the OR-CDs fluorescence emission spectra was studied. As shown in Fig. 2C, the emission peaks are shifted from 605 nm to 498 nm with decreasing polarity, corresponding to the emission colors from red to blue, respectively (from right to left in the inset of Fig. 2C). Similar to the organic fluorophore, the solvent-dependent property of OR-CDs results from dipole–dipole interactions between the solvent and OR-CDs (Ai et al., 2021; Chao et al., 2018). This phenomenon also reveals that aggregated organic fluorophores exist in the OR-CDs, which is consistent with the TGA results.

The fluorescence responses of OR-CDs to different concentrations of Cd2+ are shown in Fig. 2D. The fluorescence intensity of OR-CDs is gradually enhanced with increasing Cd2+ concentration. Notably, in the concentration range of 0.80–100 μM, the fluorescence intensity of OR-CDs shows a good linear relationship (Fig. 2E) with the Cd2+ concentration, and the detection limit is calculated to be 0.30 μM (S/N = 3). As the carbon sources SA and Aphen have no fluorescence enhancement response to Cd2+ (Fig. S9), the unique fluorescence enhancement property towards Cd2+ results from the synthesized OR-CDs. The selectivity of the resulting OR-CDs was evaluated by adding various metal ions and biomolecules. As shown in Fig. 2F, Cd2+ has a significant fluorescence enhancement effect on OR-CDs, whereas other metal ions show little influence (Fig. S10). These results reveal that OR-CDs have a high selectivity towards Cd2+compared to other transition metals. Furthermore, many potentially coexisting biomolecules also show no interference for the determination of Cd2+ (Fig. S11), proving that our OR-CDs are favorable for application in intracellular detection. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence intensity of the OR-CDs increases with increasing of Cd2+ addition, whereas the peak intensity almost decreases to its initial value upon the addition of EDTA. After cycling five times, OR-CDs still show a good response towards Cd2+, indicating that the OR-CDs have a reproducible response towards Cd2+. More importantly, the OR-CDs solution still maintains its fluorescence intensity after 40 days, which is conducive to long-term storage (Fig. S13). With increasing temperature, the fluorescence intensity of OR-CDs is also basically unchanged (Fig. S14), indicating that OR-CDs have good temperature stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT optimization of the OR-CDs structure
ce:section.ce:para: According to the FT-IR and XPS results, the amide bond in OR-CDs is formed by the carboxyl group of SA and the amino group of Aphen. However, it is difficult to determine the exact structure formed by SA and Aphen merely based on the experimental data. Hence, theoretical calculations were conducted in the present work. Since the amount of SA is much greater than that of Aphen in the preparation of OR-CDs, it is reasonable to assume that the conjugated system in the OR-CDs should be composed of one Aphen combined with one or more SA molecule. Considering that the energy of the conjugate system mainly depends on the number of conjugated rings, conjugated structures containing five to ten rings were constructed as models, which were symmetrically optimized by Gaussian 09 packages at the B3LYP/6-311G (d,p) basis set (Xu et al., 2021). The energy gaps (Eg) for five to ten conjugated rings are given in Table S1. As shown in Fig. 3A, the Eg value of the conjugated structure labeled 9–2 is 2.76 eV, which is close to that found in the experimental results (2.67 eV) (Fig. S15). Subsequently, the conjugated structure 9–2 with carboxyl functional groups was further optimized. The Eg value of the conjugated structure marked 9–2b is found to be very close to the experimental value (2.67 eV). From mass spectrometry (MS), a strong peak of at 506.98 is observed for the OR-CDs, which is consistent with the molecular ion fragment labeled 9–2b (calcd m/z 507.08) (Fig. S16). The calculated absorption spectrum for the 9–2b structure also agrees well with the measured UV-Vis absorbance spectrum for OR-CDs (Fig. S17). The 1H-nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectrum for OR-CDs (DMSO-d6) is shown in Fig. S18A. Obvious aromatic hydrogen signals are detected in the range of 7.5–8.0 ppm. The peaks at 8.4 ppm and 8.7 ppm are ascribed to the active hydrogen of HCN and CONH, respectively. The signals due to -COOH are displayed in the range of 8.9–9.2 ppm. As shown in Fig. S18B, the 13C-nuclear magnetic resonance (13C NMR) peaks in the range of 110.0–140.0 ppm correspond to the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms of OR-CDs. The carbonyl of amide appears at 161.47 ppm, and the peak observed at 172.45 ppm corresponds to the carbonyl carbon of the carboxylic group. The 1H NMR and 13C NMR signals for OR-CDs also support the proposed structure 9–2b. The molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) diagram given in the inset of Fig. S17 shows that the basic unit of OR-CDs consists of two colors: the blue color represents the positive regions and the orange color refers to the electronegative regions. Electron-rich orange regions are mainly distributed around the most electronegative center of the molecule, i.e., the N and O atoms. These regions are responsible for coordination with electron-deficient Cd2+. Therefore, the calculated structure labeled as 9–2b is believed to be the basic unit of the OR-CDs. The formation of OR-CDs can be described by the route presented in Fig. 3B, i.e., the same basic unit can be formed by the dehydration and decarburization reaction between SA and Aphen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism for the OR-CDs response to Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the sensing mechanism, experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations for OR-CDs in the presence of Cd2+ were carried out. TEM, AFM and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were used to study the morphological changes in OR-CDs after adding Cd2+. As shown in Fig. S19, the size of OR-CDs increases from 2 to 4 nm to 10–20 nm with the addition of Cd2+. Correspondingly, as shown in Fig. S20, the AFM image shows that the height of the OR-CDs increases from 1 to 2 nm to 6–15 nm with the addition of Cd2+. The DLS in Fig. S21 also shows that the hydrodynamic diameters of OR-CDs are increased from 10 to 20 nm to 60–90 nm with the addition of Cd2+. These results indicate that the addition of Cd2+ induces obvious aggregation of OR-CDs. Thus, the enhancement of the observed fluorescence in the Cd-OR-CDs can be ascribed to the Cd2+-induced AIEE mechanism. The AIEE effect was also confirmed by the addition of ethanol to the OR-CDs solution (Wang et al., 2016). Fig. S22 shows that the fluorescence intensity from OR-CDs is increased continuously with increasing ethanol fractions. After the addition of 50 μM Cd2+ to the OR-CDs solution, the attained mass signal at 620.46 belongs to the Cd-OR-CDs (calcd m/z 620.99), as shown in Fig. S23. Furthermore, many larger fragments appear in the mass spectrum for Cd-OR-CDs, indicating that Cd2+ induces the aggregation of OR-CDs. To further elucidate the enhancement mechanism, 1H NMR and 13C NMR for OR-CDs in the presence and absence of Cd2+ were measured in DMSO-d6. As shown in Fig. S24, after addition of 50 μM Cd2+, the carboxyl proton in the OR-CDs is moved from 8.9 ppm to 9.0 ppm, and the proton signal due to the amide group is shifted from 8.7 ppm to 8.8 ppm (Concellón et al., 2018). The proton peak at 8.4 ppm is changed to 8.3 ppm, which is attributed to the chemical shift of HCN (Pang et al., 2020). After the addition of 50 μM Cd2+ to the OR-CDs solution, the intensity of the carbon signal at 172.0–160.0 ppm is obviously decreased (Fig. S25). The carbonyl carbon due to the carboxylic group is shifted from 172.45 ppm to 172.27 ppm. The carbonyl signal due to amide is changed from 161.47 ppm changed to 161.66 ppm. These results reveal that the carboxyl, amide and HCN groups are involved in the chelation of Cd2+. After the addition of Cd2+, the zeta potential for the OR-CDs changed from − 14.49 mV to − 8.39 mV, suggesting the formation of Cd-OR-CDs (Fig. S26). The fluorescence lifetime of OR-CDs increases from 4.4 ns to 13.4 ns with the addition of Cd2+ (Fig. S27), suggesting the inhibition of the nonradiative process due to the chelation of Cd2+ to the OR-CDs. DFT calculations were performed to further verify the binding mode of OR-CDs and Cd2+. As shown in Fig. 4, OR-CDs can precisely accommodate electron-deficient Cd2+ by coordinating with electron-rich N and O. The sum of the radius of Cd2+ and N is 2.37 Å and that of Cd2+ and O is 2.22 Å (Burton et al., 2003; Wu et al., 1999). These figures are very close to the calculated bond lengths of Cd with N (2.34 Å) and O (2.23 Å), respectively (Table S2). The two basic units of OR-CDs are perfectly connected by Cd2+ via the formation of five coordination bonds (Fig. S28), resulting in the aggregation of OR-CDs. As shown in Fig. S29, when the radius of metal ion M is less than Cd2+ (e.g., Cu2+), theoretical calculations show that M can only form two coordination bonds with one basic unit but cannot connect the two basic units. When the radius of the metal ion M is larger than that of Cd2+(e.g., Hg2+), M forms two coordination bonds with the two basic units. Additionally, because of the large ionic radius, the two basic units are far away from each other, and thus cannot form stable OR-CDs aggregates. For the case of Zn2+, although it has an ionic radius similar to as that of Cd2+, our calculation results show that Zn2+ can only form three coordination bonds between the two basic units, leading to the formation of less stable OR-CDs aggregates. These results indicate that the specific structure of OR-CDs allows them to only accommodate Cd2+ in the formation of stable aggregates, which is further manifested by the binding constants of M-OR-CDs (M stands for metal ions). As shown in Table S3, the binding constant of Cd-OR-CDs (5.24 × 102) is the highest among all the studied transition metals, which is accountable for the high selectivity of OR-CDs towards Cd2+. These results indicate that the structure of the OR-CDs matches the size of the Cd2+ ion, resulting in the specific response to Cd2+. In contrast, other metals exhibit weak coordination ability with OR-CDs due to an inappropriate ionic radius and thus cannot induce distinct aggregation of OR-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro imaging and apoptosis analysis
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the obtained OR-CDs have two advantages as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cd2+ in living cells: (1) Long fluorescence wavelength emission (605 nm) overcomes the interference of cell background signals. (2) Good water solubility is conducive to compatibility with cells. To validate the practical use of the OR-CDs, a preliminary assessment of cytotoxicity was carried out by CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. S30, after A549 cells were incubated with 0.01–0.04 mg/mL OR-CDs for 24 h, almost 100.0% of the cells survived. Even when the concentration was as high as 0.20 mg/mL, a survival rate over 92.0% was still observed, indicating that the OR-CDs have very low cytotoxicity. Subsequently, living cell images were collected by a confocal microscope with excitation at a wavelength of 458 nm. As shown in Fig. 5A, A549 cells treated only with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs show weak red fluorescence. After A549 cells were cultured simultaneously with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs and 20.0 μM Cd2+, the red channel showed substantially enhanced fluorescence. Liver, kidney, and lung cells corresponding to HepG2, 293 T and A549 cells, respectively, were used to evaluate the practical application of OR-CDs. The fluorescence intensity of intracellular OR-CDs (at 605 nm) was measured by a full-function microplate detector (Synergy H4, America). As shown in Fig. S31, the fluorescence intensity of OR-CDs in HepG2, 273T and A549 cells increased gradually with increasing Cd2+ concentration. These results indicate that OR-CDs can successfully image and sense Cd2+ in living cells in turn-on mode.

When Cd2+ is less than 10 μM, cell death is not obvious in most cells (Shimada et al., 1998). However, a large number of studies have shown that a high concentration of Cd2+ can induce production of abnormal reactive oxygen species (ROS), which results in cell apoptosis and necrosis (Pi et al., 2018). To alleviate cadmium poisoning, EDTA, penicillamine and dimercaprol are frequently utilized as antidotes (Rafati Rahimzadeh et al., 2017). Although these antidotes can effectively decrease Cd content in body fluids, they also chelate other metal ions that are essential for normal cells. Hence, selective chelating of Cd2+ is critical to alleviate cadmium poisoning. The alleviation of cadmium poisoning with OR-CDs was studied by flow cytometry. Untreated A549 cells were used as a control group in Fig. 5B. After the A549 cells were cultured with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs for 24 h, 94.0% of the cells remained in intact (Fig. 5C), indicating that OR-CDs have good biocompatibility, which is consistent with the cytotoxicity results. After the A549 cells were treated with only with 20 μM Cd2+ for 24 h, the percentages of necrotic and apoptotic cells were 37.9% and 45.7%, respectively (Fig. 5D). After incubating A549 cells with 0.10 mg/mL OR-CDs and 20 μM Cd2+ for the same time, the fraction of necrotic and apoptotic cells was decreased to 11.5% and 10.5%, respectively (Fig. 5E), which demonstrated that OR-CDs can significantly reduce the cell damage caused by Cd2+ (Fig. 5F). As shown in Table S4, compared with the probes that have been reported in the literature, the OR-CDs prepared in the present work exhibits a long emission wavelength, excellent biocompatibility and high selectivity for the detection of Cd2+.

In summary, highly efficient fluorescence OR-CDs with long wavelength emission were successfully prepared via a simple calcination method using SA and Aphen as carbon sources. Comprehensive experimental characterizations and theoretical calculations have demonstrated that (1) the OR-CDs are composed of a basic unit of nine aromatic rings, which is responsible for the observed orange–red emissive fluorescence at a wavelength of 605 nm; (2) the sterically restricted chelation of OR-CDs with Cd2+ leads to the AIEE, which is accountable for the selective turn-on fluorescence response to Cd2+; (3) the formation of stable aggregates between the OR-CDs with Cd2+ is manifested by the high binding constant of 5.24 × 102, which is substantially greater than that observed for other transition metals; (4) the OR-CDs also exhibit excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, and stable and reproducible fluorescence performance over extended storage times; and (5) the specific chelating ability of OR-CDs also shows potential for application for alleviating cadmium poisoning in living organisms.

The present study demonstrates the development of a sensitive and selective fluorescence probe for Cd2+ detection; more importantly, the sensing mechanism exploited in this work should be widely applicable for the development of other fluorescence probes for environmental monitoring and cell imaging.

Yalan Xu: Investigation, Data acquisition, Writing – original draft. Chan Wang: Formal analysis. Tao Jiang: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization. Guoxia Ran: Formal analysis. Qijun Song: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Natural National Science Foundation of China (51973083), National First-Class Discipline Program of Food Science and Technology (JUFSTR20180301). We would like to acknowledge the work of Central Laboratory, School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jhazmat.2021.128092.

ACS Sens.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xiao, M., Liu, Z., Xu, N., Jiang, L., Yang, M., Yi, C., 2020. A smartphone-based sensing system for on-site quantitation of multiple heavy metal ions using fluorescent carbon nanodots-based microarrays. ACS Sens. 5, 870–878.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Xu et al., 2021

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phosphate-assisted transformation of methylene blue to red-emissive carbon dots with enhanced singlet oxygen generation for photodynamic therapy
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xu, Y., Wang, C., Ran, G., Chen, D., Pang, Q., Song, Q., 2021. Phosphate-assisted transformation of methylene blue to red-emissive carbon dots with enhanced singlet oxygen generation for photodynamic therapy. ACS Appl. Nano Mater. 4, 4820–4828.
